description.summary: Legendary creature with the head, wings, and talons of an eagle combined with the body of a lion, symbolizing divine power and guardianship.
description.full: The griffin is a legendary creature combining the features of the king of beasts (lion) and the king of birds (eagle). Depicted with an eagle's head, beak, and wings, and a lion's body and tail, the griffin often has eagle's talons on its forelegs. In ancient mythology, griffins guarded treasures and sacred places, were sacred to Apollo, and their nests were said to contain gold. The griffin likely originated in the Ancient Near East over 5,000 years ago and spread through Scythian, Greek, and Roman cultures. A popular theory suggests griffins were inspired by Protoceratops fossils in gold-mining regions of ancient Scythia, which ancient peoples may have interpreted as the remains of these legendary guardians.

etymology.origin: From Greek 'gryps' (γρύψ) possibly from 'grypós' (γρυπός) meaning 'curved' or 'hooked' (referring to beak)
etymology.first_use: Ancient Near East (circa 3000 BCE in art)

related_phenomena.name: Sphinx
related_phenomena.description: Lion body with human or ram head

related_phenomena.name: Lamassu
related_phenomena.description: Assyrian winged bull or lion with human head

physical.description: Eagle head, wings, and forelegs; lion body and hindlegs

components.head: Eagle head with sharp, curved beak

physical.size: Typically depicted as larger than a lion

features.description: Sharp beak, keen eyes, sometimes ear tufts
Forelegs often depicted as eagle talons rather than lion paws

abilities.description: Powerful flight capability with eagle wings
Combined strength of lion and eagle
Fierce protection of treasures and sacred places
Nests contain gold; guards gold deposits

range.description: Ancient Near East origins, spread through Mediterranean and Europe

first_recorded.source: Ancient Near Eastern art and seals

origins.cultural_roots: Ancient Near East (circa 3000 BCE), possibly inspired by Protoceratops fossils in Scythian gold-mining regions
origins.earliest_accounts: Egyptian and Mesopotamian art (3000+ BCE), Scythian gold decorations
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to treasure guardianship, solar deities (Apollo), divine protection

timeline.significance: Appear in Ancient Near Eastern art
Griffin mythology enters Greek culture via trade with Scythia
Griffins associated with sun god Apollo, guarding his treasures
Griffins in bestiaries representing Christ (divine and earthly nature)

history.modern_era: Griffins remain popular in heraldry, corporate logos, architecture, and fantasy literature. No biological claims exist.

famous_examples.name: Griffins of Apollo
famous_examples.description: Drew the chariot of Apollo, guarded his gold in the land of the Hyperboreans

famous_examples.name: Scythian gold griffins
famous_examples.description: Griffins guarding gold in the Altai Mountains, fought with Arimaspi (one-eyed people)

historical.name: Archaeological depictions
historical.description: Griffin imagery in Ancient Near Eastern, Scythian, Greek, and Roman art
historical.analysis: Widespread artistic motif, no biological evidence

mythological.name: Divine power symbol
mythological.description: Griffin represents combination of divine (eagle) and earthly (lion) power

historical_basis.name: Protoceratops fossil theory
historical_basis.description: Ancient gold miners in Scythia discovered Protoceratops fossils and interpreted them as griffin remains
evidence_cited: Protoceratops fossils common in Scythian gold-mining regions

historical_basis.name: Symbolic chimera
historical_basis.description: Purely symbolic combination of powerful animals without biological inspiration

symbolism.christian: Dual nature of Christ (divine and earthly)

literature: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland by Lewis Carroll (Gryphon character)
Harry Potter series by J.K. Rowling (Gryffindor house symbol)
Percy Jackson series by Rick Riordan

sources.title: The First Fossil Hunters: Dinosaurs, Mammoths, and Myth in Greek and Roman Times
The Origin of the Greek Harpē Sword
